fields.name: 06. Debate Research: Fast facts for strong arguments
fields.content-01:
Introduction (5 minutes)

  • Explain the objectives of the workshop and the importance of research in debates.
  • Discuss key elements of good research: relevance, credibility, and clarity.
  • Divide students into small groups of 3-4 and assign each group a debate topic.

‍

Research (15 minutes)

‍

  • Each group uses their devices to research their assigned topic.
  • Encourage students to divide tasks within their groups
  • Monitor and assist groups as needed, offering guidance on credible sources.

‍

Presentation (20 minutes)

‍

  • Each group has 2-3 minutes to present their findings to the class.
  • Presentations should include:
    • A brief overview of the topic.
    • Key arguments or findings.
  • Encourage a short Q&A after each presentation for peer feedback and discussion.

‍

Reflection (5 minutes)

‍

  • Discuss what students learned from the activity:
    • What was challenging about researching quickly?
    • How did they decide which information to include?
  • Highlight the importance of evaluating sources and presenting information effectively.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • To enhance students' research skills, focusing on speed and accuracy.
  • To encourage critical thinking by evaluating the relevance and credibility of information.
  • To build confidence in presenting research findings clearly and effectively.
  • To introduce industry-relevant topics for discussion, fostering engagement and practical learning.
fields.content-03:
Learning Outcomes

‍

By the end of the workshop, students will be able to:

  • Quickly research and gather relevant information on a given topic.
  • Assess the credibility and reliability of online sources.
  • Synthesize information into clear, concise arguments.
  • Present their findings effectively in a group setting.
fields.content-04:
Materials and Tools

‍

  • Devices with internet access (smartphones, tablets, or computers).
  • A list of specific, industry-relevant debate topics for each group (examples provided at the end of this lesson).
  • A timer to manage research and presentation phases.
  • Optional: A flipchart or whiteboard for summarizing key points from each group.
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Observations during the research phase: Are students effectively dividing tasks and collaborating?
  • Quality of presentations: Are arguments supported with credible evidence?
  • Engagement during Q&A and reflection discussions.

‍

Tips and Tricks

‍

  • Use industry-relevant topics that are specific and engaging to vocational students.
  • Provide a list of reliable websites or sources if students struggle to identify credible information.
fields.extra-text:
ATTACHMENT: Examples of research topics

‍

  1. The impact of automation on job availability in specific industries (e.g., manufacturing, retail, healthcare).
  2. Sustainable practices in local businesses.
  3. Apprenticeship programs versus traditional classroom education in terms of career outcomes.
  4. Development and adoption of electric vehicles and their influence on the automotive industry.
  5. The effectiveness of social media marketing for promoting small and medium-sized businesses.
  6. Renewable energy industries and the job opportunities they create for vocational students.
  7. The use of virtual reality for training in vocational fields like welding, healthcare, and aviation.
  8. The environmental impact of fast fashion and sustainable alternatives in the textile industry.
